Because of the rest time after the Li plating, the plated Li might have intercalated or chemically reacted, which had positive effects on the safety behavior of the cell during TR.

claims.claim: No hydrogen fluoride (HF) was identified by FTIR after the first venting or thermal runaway in overtemperature experiments on fresh or aged NMC-graphite pouch cells tested in the described reactor setup.
claims.quote: in the presented results no hydrogen ﬂuoride (HF) was identiﬁed with the FTIR spectrometer inside the FTIR gas measurement chamber after the ﬁrst venting or the TR.

claims.claim: At 90–100% SOC, NMC pouch cells with 32 Ah capacity generated a maximum pressure of 7.05 ± 0.5 bar, while 10 Ah cells at the same SOC generated 4.1 ± 0.7 bar, in a 100 dm3 airtight reactor vessel.
claims.quote: At the range of 90–100% SOC, the pmax values of the 10 Ah cells and 32 Ah cells were 4.1 ± 0.7 and 7.05 ± 0.5 bar, respectively.

claims.claim: In three tests at 90–100% SOC, maximum pressures higher than 7 bar occurred that cannot be explained by gas release alone, indicating that released gases underwent ignition inside the reactor vessel.
claims.quote: In three tests (Tests #11, #13 and #14) with the range of 90–100% SOC, maximum pressures higher than 7 bars occurred. Pressure values of this magnitude cannot be explained by the increase in the number of moles added to the gas phase due to gas release. In these tests, obviously, the gas released from the batteries into the RV underwent a gas explosion.

claims.claim: At SOC levels of 30–50%, the peak reaction temperature during thermal runaway was 642 ± 178 K and maximum pressure was 1.2 ± 0.15 bar, whereas at 90–100% SOC for single 10 Ah cells, peak reaction temperature was 976 ± 90 K and maximum pressure was 4.1 ± 0.7 bar.
The onset temperature of NMC pouch cells had an inverse relationship with SOC level, with Tonset decreasing as SOC increased in Series 2 tests.
claims.quote: The results of the 10 Ah cells tested indicated the Tonset had an inverse relationship with the SOC levels of the LIBs.

claims.claim: The highest normalized gas volume released was 3.2 L/Ah from a single 32 Ah NMC cell at 90–100% SOC, while the lowest was 1.5 L/Ah at 30–50% SOC, demonstrating SOC dependency of gas release.
claims.quote: The highest release of gas was obtained from the 32 Ah cells with a range of 90–100% SOC, with the least at a range of 30–50% SOC (1.5 L/Ah).

claims.claim: The determination of the total number of moles of gas released during thermal runaway may depend on the apparatus volume, because oxygen availability in the closed reactor vessel limits the completeness of combustion reactions.
claims.quote: the determination of the total number of moles of gas released might, to some extent, depend on the apparatus.

claims.claim: CO2 measurements using FTIR did not give realistic results due to strong spectral interference with carbonyl compounds released during thermal runaway, which have absorption regions at close wavenumbers.
claims.quote: Carbon dioxide measurements did not give realistic results because of strong interference with some of the carbonyl compounds that may have been released at TR. Technically, these compounds have a close wavenumber of the absorption region.

claims.claim: The TR critical temperatures of the four battery samples are: NCM622—154.7°C, NCM811—120.8°C, NCM9/0.5/0.5—130.8°C, and LFP—145.2°C.
claims.quote: The TR critical temperatures of the four battery samples are: NCM622—154.7 °C, NCM811—120.8 °C, NCM9/0.5/0.5—130.8 °C, LFP—145.2 °C.

claims.claim: The maximum TR surface temperatures are: NCM9/0.5/0.5—842.1°C, NCM811—803.4°C, NCM622—559.1°C, and LFP—360.9°C.
claims.quote: the temperature of the NCM9/0.5/0.5 battery is the highest during TR, reaching 842.1 °C; the maximum temperature of the NCM811 battery is 803.4 ℃, the maximum temperature of the NCM622 battery is 559.1℃, and the maximum temperature of the LFP battery is only 360.9 °C.

claims.claim: The voltage drop of the battery during lateral heating occurs 2–8 seconds before the sudden temperature rise that marks thermal runaway onset.
claims.quote: the voltage drops of the three batteries occurred two to eight seconds before the sudden temperature rise.

claims.claim: The mass loss rates before and after TR for NCM622, NCM811, NCM9/0.5/0.5, and LFP batteries are 38.12%, 37.80%, 72.89%, and 22.80%, respectively.
claims.quote: the mass loss rate before and after TR were 38.12%, 37.80%, 72.89%, and 22.80%, respectively.

claims.claim: NCM622, NCM811, and NCM9/0.5/0.5 batteries eject gas and black solid particles during TR, while LFP batteries eject unreacted electrolytes during TR.
claims.quote: the NCM622 battery, NCM811 battery, and NCM9/0.5/0.5 battery mainly eject black solid particles after TR, while the LFP batteries eject unreacted electrolytes.

claims.claim: The gas production of NCM622 is 3.93 mol, NCM811 is 12.01 mol, NCM9/0.5/0.5 is 17.14 mol, and LFP is 10.3 mol during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the gas production of the NCM622 battery is 3.93 mol, the gas production of the NCM811 battery is 12.01 mol, the gas production of the NCM9/0.5/0.5 battery is 17.14 mol, and the gas production of the LFP battery is 10.3 mol.

claims.claim: The LFP battery generates less gas after TR compared to NCM batteries, attributed to the solid covalent P–O bond in the LiFePO4 cathode reducing oxygen release.
claims.quote: the LFP battery generates less gas after TR, which is attributed to the solid covalent P–O bond in the cathode of the lithium iron phosphate battery, reducing oxygen release.

claims.claim: CO concentration in TR gas is positively related to battery energy density with a fitting coefficient of 0.39.
claims.quote: The fitting curve shows that the increase of CO concentration is positively related to the energy density, with a coefficient of 0.39.

claims.claim: H2 concentration in TR gas is negatively related to battery energy density with a fitting coefficient of −0.28.
claims.quote: the concentration change of H2 is negatively related to the energy density, with a coefficient of −0.28.

claims.claim: HF was not detected in the TR gas under the nitrogen inert atmosphere used in this experiment, because the water content in the chamber was too low for the intermediate substance PF5 to react and generate HF.
claims.quote: HF is not detected in this experiment because there is an inert nitrogen atmosphere in the test chamber. Some studies have shown that HF will not be formed under the water content of 10 ppm... the water content in the chamber is shallow due to the replacement of inert gas, and the intermediate substance PF5 that generates HF does not react with it.

claims.claim: The flammability concentration range of NCM622 TR gas is the largest at 54% with a lower flammability limit of 7%, while the NCM9/0.5/0.5 TR gas has the smallest range at 41% with a lower flammability limit of 8.2%.
claims.quote: The flammability concentration range of the NCM622 battery is the largest, 54%, and the lower flammability limit is 7%. The flammability concentration range of the NCM9/0.5/0.5 battery is the most negligible, 41%, and the lower flammability limit is 8.2%.

claims.claim: The lower flammability limit of TR gas from the LFP battery is the lowest among the four samples at 6%, when accounting for the effect of battery electrolyte eruption.
claims.quote: the lower flammability limit of the LFP battery is the lowest among the four samples, 6%, taking into account the effect of battery electrolyte eruption.

claims.claim: With increasing battery energy density in the NCM system, the flammability limit range of TR gas is gradually reduced and the lower flammability limit is gradually increased, indicating reduced deflagration risk at higher energy densities.
claims.quote: with the increase of the battery energy density, the flammability limit range of the gas generated by TR is gradually reduced, especially in the NCM system. The lower flammability limit is gradually increased with the energy density increase. The above analysis shows that increasing the battery energy density will reduce the battery deflagration risk from the perspective of the gas deflagration limit.

claims.claim: Normalized gas production is positively related to energy density, and NCM system batteries produce more gas per unit capacity than LFP batteries under the same battery capacity conditions.
claims.quote: the normalized gas production results after the TR show that the gas production of the battery is positively related to the energy density. Moreover, the normalized gas production results of the NCM622 battery, the NCM811 battery, and the NCM9/0.5/0.5 battery are higher than those of LFP battery, indicating that the NCM system's gas production is higher than that of the LFP system under the same battery capacity.

claims.claim: A large amount of HF will be produced when water content exceeds 300 ppm, while HF will not be formed under water content of 10 ppm.
claims.quote: Some studies have shown that HF will not be formed under the water content of 10 ppm, and a large amount of hydrofluo ric acid will be produced under 300 ppm.

claims.claim: For NMC pouch cells (2.5 Ah), the maximum pressure during thermal runaway increased with increasing SOC, from 1.2 ± 0.04 bar at 0% SOC to 3.2 ± 0.5 bar at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the pmax increased with increasing SOC. The highest pmax from test series 1 (3.2 ± 0.5 bar) was observed at 100% SOC, and the lowest value (1.2 ± 0.04 bar) was observed at 0% SOC.

claims.claim: For NMC pouch cells at SOC levels ≤ 50%, thermal abuse resulted only in gas release with cell swelling observed, but no thermal explosion.
claims.quote: the pouch cells tested at SOC level ≤ 50% showed no sign of thermal explosion. However, cell swelling was observed.

claims.claim: At SOC levels ≥ 80%, thermal-runaway-induced explosion of NMC pouch cells was evident at 100% SOC, with thermal expansion and rupture occurring at approximately 80% SOC.
claims.quote: At SOC values of approximately 80%, higher Treac values were measured. The effects of the high Treac and associated Pmax values lead to a thermal expansion and therefore rapture of the cells. However, thermal-runaway-induced explosion of the cells was evident at 100% SOC.

claims.claim: The maximum gas volume released from 32 Ah NMC cells during thermal runaway was 102 ± 4 L, with a normalized gas volume of 3.2 ± 0.1 L/Ah.
claims.quote: In test series 2, the highest level of gas release (102 ± 4 L) was generated from tests #27–#29 (32 Ah NMC cells), with a normalized gas volume of 3.2 ± 0.1 L/Ah.

claims.claim: For 2.5 Ah NMC pouch cells, an insignificant amount of gas emission (≤1 L) was released at SOC levels ≤ 30%, while the highest amount (6.5 ± 0.4 L) was measured at 100% SOC, representing more than three times the release at 50% SOC (1.9 L).
claims.quote: In test series 1, an insignificant amount of gas emission (⪅1 L) was released at SOC levels ≤ 30%, while the highest amount of gas (6.5 ± 0.4 L) was measured at 100% SOC. The volume of gas produced at 100% SOC was more than three times larger than the release at 50% SOC (i.e., 1.9 L).

claims.claim: Applying Le Chatelier's law to the gas mixture released from NMC LIBs during thermal runaway yielded a lower explosion limit (LEL) of 7 vol% and an upper explosion limit (UEL) of 31 vol%.
claims.quote: Applying Le Chatelier's law based on the lowest values measured for the individual components (see Table 6) yields a lower explosion limit (LEL) of 7% in the volume fraction for the gas mixture and an upper explosion limit (UEL) of 31% in volume.

claims.claim: CO, CH4, and C2H4 released during thermal runaway of NMC cells reached concentrations within their individual explosible ranges, with maximum measured concentrations of 16.85 vol%, 7.6 vol%, and 7.86 vol%, respectively.
claims.quote: Of the five studied flammable gases, the maximum concentrations of carbon monoxide (16.85 vol%), methane (7.6 vol%), and ethylene (7.86 vol%) were identified to be within their explosible range.

claims.claim: A pyrobubble filter achieved up to 94% removal of HCN and up to 100% removal of CO2 from gas emissions released during thermal runaway of NMC LIBs.
claims.quote: The investigation revealed that the pyrobubbles filter was highly effect in the removal of HCN (up to 94% removal) and CO2 (up to 100% removal).

claims.claim: At high SOC values, the removal efficiency of the pyrobubble filter for CO and C2H6 was lower, attributed to saturation of active sites by the near-complete adsorption of highly concentrated CO2.
claims.quote: In the case of the gas components such as CH4, C2H4, and C2H6, the removal rate was generally lower at a high-SOC states. After the near-complete removal of highly concentrated CO2, there were likely fewer active sites available for component removal, resulting in the observed outcome.

claims.claim: The onset time of thermal runaway triggered by overcharge has an inverse relationship with the overcharge C-rate; at C-rate = 2 the onset was approximately 1640 s, compared to approximately 720 s at C-rate = 4.
claims.quote: In test series 3, the thermal runaway occurred in tests #30 and #31 in about 1640 s and 720 s, respectively. This indicates that the onset of thermal runaway is dependent on the overcharge rate (C rate)... The results indicate that the onset of thermal runaway has an inverse relationship with the overcharge rate.

claims.claim: Overcharge-triggered thermal runaway produced higher cell surface temperatures (Treac = 750–978 K) compared to overheating-triggered thermal runaway of the same 2.5 Ah NMC cells (Treac ≤ 529 K), due to additional electrical energy introduced into the cell.
claims.quote: the measured Treac values from test series 3 (test #30: Treac = 750 K at C-rate = 2 and test #31: Treac = 978 K at C-rate = 4) are higher than those measured in test series 1 (tests #17–#20: Treac ≤ 529 K). One explanation is that the overcharge procedure (test series 3) introduced additional electrical energy into the cell before the thermal runaway was triggered.

claims.claim: No CO2 was detected at SOC levels of 0% and 30% for 2.5 Ah NMC pouch cells during thermal abuse, indicating that the reaction is less complete at low SOC.
claims.quote: No CO2 was detected at SOC levels of 0% (tests #01–#04) and 30% (#05–#08), which means that the reaction is less complete during thermal abuse.

claims.claim: A cell surface temperature exceeding 750 K during thermal runaway can provide the necessary condition for ignition of the ejected gas mixture from NMC LIBs.
claims.quote: Based on tests #21–#29, a Treac > 750 K could provide the necessary condition for the ignition of the ejected gas mixture.

claims.claim: The organic carbonate electrolyte in LIBs decomposes in the temperature range of 363–493 K, producing gases including CO, CO2, CH4, C2H4, C2H6, C2H5F, H2, and HF.
claims.quote: In a temperature range of 363–493 K, the organic carbonate electrolyte decomposes, and a reaction between Li and electrolytes occurs to produce various organic and inorganic compounds. Some main gases produced include carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), ethylene (C2H4), ethane (C2H6), fluoroethane (C2H5F), hydrogen (H2), and hydrogen fluoride (HF).

claims.claim: HCN concentration in untreated gas emissions from NMC LIBs during thermal runaway ranged from 34 ppm to 200 ppm and was clearly higher at high SOC levels.
claims.quote: The concentration of HCN from the untreated gas mixture was identified to be between 34 ppm and 200 ppm and was clearly higher at high SOC levels.

claims.claim: For a 280 Ah LFP battery at 100% SOC under gas production conditions, the maximum front surface temperature during thermal runaway reached 520.6°C and the back surface reached 421.7°C.
claims.quote: The battery maximum surface temperatures of the front and back are 520.6°C, 421.7°C, respectively.

claims.claim: For a 280 Ah LFP battery at 50% SOC under gas production conditions, the maximum front surface temperature during thermal runaway was 434.9°C and the back surface was 307.3°C.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature of the battery front and back surface was 434.9°C and 307.3°C respectively.

claims.claim: Under the same SOC, the battery surface temperature under gas production conditions and flame conditions is almost the same, indicating that gas combustion has a small effect on battery surface temperature.
claims.quote: when the battery was under the same SOC, the battery surface temperature under the two conditions was almost the same. The flame appears above the battery and has a certain jet velocity, and the flame radiation is not effective in heating the battery itself. Therefore, the gas combustion has a small effect on the battery surface temperature.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway of a 280 Ah LFP battery at 100% SOC produces a flame peak temperature exceeding 900°C, while the gas temperature under non-ignition conditions at 100% SOC reaches only 325.7°C at 5 cm above the safety vent.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature of the flame above the battery with 50% SOC can reach about 750°C, while the temperature of the battery with 100% SOC was higher and the peak temperature can reach more than 900°C... the maximum temperature of the gas was higher, which was 325.7°C at 5 cm.

claims.claim: The internal reaction is defined as irreversible when the temperature rise rate at the battery back surface exceeds 0.5°C/s; at 50% SOC this condition persists for 80 s, while at 100% SOC it persists for 200 s.
claims.quote: the reaction inside the battery is defined as irreversible when the temperature rise rate is greater than 0.5°C/s. For the 50% SOC cell, the temperature rise rate is greater than 0.5°C/s for a duration of 80 s and 200 s for 100% SOC cell.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the peak mass loss rates during thermal runaway under gas production conditions were 12.9 g/s and 15.25 g/s, compared to only 2.3 g/s and 1.25 g/s at 50% SOC.
claims.quote: The internal reaction of the 50% SOC cell was slower, corresponding to two smaller peaks of 2.3 g/s and 1.25 g/s. The capacity of 100% SOC cell was relatively high, and the thermal runaway process was quite severe, with two peak mass loss rates of 12.9 g/s and 15.25 g/s, respectively.

claims.claim: When the safety vent fully opens during thermal runaway of a 280 Ah LFP battery, the mass loss rate reaches approximately 110 g/s as gas and electrolyte are ejected.
claims.quote: When the internal pressure reached the pressure value of the safety vent, the safety vent was broken, and much gas and electrolyte were ejected, resulting in linear mass decline as shown in Fig. 9. The mass loss rate in this process was about 110 g/s.

claims.claim: The total heat released from combustion of the 50% SOC 280 Ah LFP battery was 20.79 MJ, compared to 25.97 MJ for the 100% SOC battery, a difference of only 5.18 MJ, attributed to the longer thermal runaway and flame duration at 50% SOC.
claims.quote: the total heat production from combustion was 20.79 MJ. For the battery with 100% SOC...the total heat production from combustion was 25.97 MJ. The combustion total heat of the 50% SOC battery was only 5.18 MJ smaller than that of the 100% SOC battery due to the longer thermal runaway duration and flame duration for the 50% SOC battery.

claims.claim: Among the four measured gases (CO, CH4, C2H4, CO2) produced during thermal runaway of a 280 Ah LFP battery, CO2 accounted for 51.2% and CO accounted for 22.9% of the measured gas composition.
claims.quote: Among the four gases, carbon dioxide accounted for up to 51.2%, followed by carbon monoxide accounting for 22.9%.

claims.claim: The FTIR spectrometer used in the experiment cannot measure hydrogen, so hydrogen concentration was not analyzed, and the reported CO2 proportion of 51.2% does not represent the proportion among all gas components including hydrogen.
claims.quote: Since the Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer cannot measure hydrogen, the concentration of hydrogen was not analyzed in this section... Since a large amount of hydrogen was also produced in the thermal runaway process, the proportion of carbon dioxide shown in Fig. 7(d) was not the proportion of all gas components.

claims.claim: The 280 Ah LFP battery contains four roll cores internally; when one side is heated, the roll core closest to the heating plate undergoes thermal runaway first, and heat conduction subsequently triggers thermal runaway in the remaining roll cores.
claims.quote: We disassembled the battery and found that there were four roll cores inside the battery. When one side of the battery is heated at a high temperature, the roll core close to the heating plate is triggered to TR, and the other roll cores will be triggered thermal runaway under the effect of heat conduction.

claims.claim: Gas temperature at both 50% SOC and 100% SOC conditions did not reach 100°C at a measurement point 40 cm above the battery safety vent during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The temperature of the gas generated by the battery with 50% SOC and 100% SOC did not reach 100°C when the measuring point is 40 cm away from the battery safety vent.

claims.claim: Under ignition conditions, the mass loss rate when the safety vent opened was 69.9 g/s for 50% SOC and 92.9 g/s for 100% SOC, lower than the ~110 g/s observed under gas production conditions, because some electrolyte and gas burned at the safety vent opening rather than being ejected.
claims.quote: the mass loss rate was low when the safety vent was opened, and the mass loss rate of 50% SOC and 100% SOC was 69.9 g/s and 92.9 g/s respectively. This was because the ignition operation was carried out when the safety vent opening. Some electrolytes and gas were not completely ejected but burned out at the opening of the battery safety vent.

claims.claim: In the absence of an external ignition source, the accumulation of large quantities of combustible gas from LFP battery thermal runaway presents risks of toxicity, asphyxiation, and explosion.
claims.quote: In the absence of an external source of ignition, the accumulation of large quantities of gas will cause the risk of toxicity, asphyxiation and explosion.

claims.claim: Overcharging causes a decrease in the Lower Explosion Limit (LEL) of thermal runaway gas in Li-ion batteries, with the overall change not exceeding 2% across the experimental overcharge gradient.
claims.quote: Overcharging causes a decrease in the LEL of thermal runaway gas, and there is a significant difference in the LEL variation of thermal runaway gas under different charge and discharge temperature environments (CTE). Since the experimental setting of the overcharge gradient is small, the change of LEL value is also small, and the overall change is not more than 2%.

claims.claim: At a charge/discharge ambient temperature of 40°C, overcharge decreases the LEL of thermal runaway gas by 0.32%, which is greater than the 0.19% decrease observed at 30°C.
claims.quote: In the charge/discharge ambient temperature of 30°C, the LEL decreases by 0.19% with the increase of the cutoff voltage, while in the charge/discharge environment of 40°C, the overcharge decreases the LEL by 0.32%.

claims.claim: The lowest thermal runaway gas LEL occurs in batteries charged and discharged at 40°C, and for charge/discharge ambient temperatures between 40°C and 60°C, the LEL increases with increasing temperature.
claims.quote: The LEL of the battery charged and discharged in the ambient temperature of 40°C is the lowest, and the overcharge has the most obvious effect on the LEL of the battery thermal runaway gas. In the charge/discharge ambient temperature of 40–60°C, the LEL increases with the increase of temperature.

claims.claim: The decrease in LEL of thermal runaway gas is caused by a decrease in CO2 content and an increase in vaporized DMC content in the gas mixture, where the increase in vaporized DMC is attributed to lithium precipitation in the negative electrode material.
claims.quote: The decrease of thermal runaway gas LEL is due to the decrease of CO2 content in the gas and the increase of vaporized Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DMC) content, and the increase of vaporized DMC content in the gas mixture is due to the precipitation of lithium in the negative electrode material.

claims.claim: At a 40°C CTE, the percentage of gaseous DMC among thermal runaway gases is 4% higher than at 30°C for normally charged batteries (4.2 V), and 20% higher under overcharge conditions at a cutoff voltage of 4.4 V.
claims.quote: the percentage of gaseous DMC among the thermal runaway gases of the Li-ion battery at 40°C CTE is 4% higher than that at 30°C, as shown in Fig. 4 (4.2 V). The gas increment is more pronounced under overcharge conditions, with 20% higher at a cutoff voltage of 4.4 V.

claims.claim: The overcharge cycle leads to thickening of the SEI membrane, causing incomplete CO2 production during thermal runaway and a resulting decrease in CO2 content with a concurrent increase in CO content.
claims.quote: The overcharge cycle leads to the thickening of the SEI membrane, and the heat does not react completely to produce CO2 in the thermal runaway stage. The decrease in CO2 content and the increase in CO content may come from the incomplete reaction of sub-stable substances and the reaction between electrolyte and lithium salt.

claims.claim: The LEL of CO is 12%, which is significantly higher than that of other combustible hydrocarbons (1.5%–5%), so the decrease in gas mixture LEL is attributed primarily to decreased CO2 content and increased combustible hydrocarbon proportions rather than increased CO.
claims.quote: the LEL of CO is 12%, which is significantly higher than that of other combustible hydrocarbons (1.5%–5%), and according to Eqs. (3) and (4), it is demonstrated that the decrease in the LEL of the gas mixture is due to the decrease in the content of CO2 and the increase in the type of combustible hydrocarbons as well as the proportion of their components.

claims.claim: The exothermic enthalpy (ΔH) of the anode material mixture with electrolyte decreases with increasing cutoff voltage: 25.35 J/g at 4.2 V, 15.13 J/g at 4.3 V, and 10.11 J/g at 4.4 V, indicating less electrolyte reacts with anode material as overcharge degree increases.
claims.quote: The ΔH of exotherm of the Li-ion battery anode material mixture with a cutoff voltage of 4.2 V is 25.34802 J/g, ΔH of 4.3 V is 15.12939 J/g, and ΔH of 4.4 V is 10.11289 J/g. The amount of exotherm decreases with increasing cutoff voltage, indicating that less electrolyte reacts with the anode material as the degree of overcharge increases.

claims.claim: Increasing charge/discharge ambient temperature from 30°C to 60°C increases the exothermic ΔH of the anode material mixture, indicating more electrolyte reacts with Li+ at higher temperatures, which reduces vaporized DMC content in thermal runaway gas.
claims.quote: the ΔH of each mixture group increases with the increase of temperature, indicating that the electrolyte reacting with Li+ increases in the high-temperature environment, which eventually leads to the decrease of the amount of vaporized DMC in the thermal runaway gas with the increase of ambient temperature.

claims.claim: The negative electrode material of a Li-ion battery with a cutoff voltage of 4.4 V exhibits two weight loss steps at 96°C and 665°C during thermogravimetric analysis, whereas batteries at 4.2 V and 4.3 V show significant weight loss only at 747°C and 722°C respectively, indicating overcharging decreases thermal stability of the negative electrode material.
claims.quote: the negative electrode materials of Li-ion batteries with cutoff voltages of 4.2 V and 4.3 V experience a significant weight loss at 722°C and 747°C, respectively, and the negative electrode materials of Li-ion batteries with cutoff voltages of 4.4 V experience two weight loss steps at 96°C and 665°C. It proves that the negative electrode material of the lithium battery in normal use only undergoes melting reaction at 700°C or above, and overcharging decreases the thermal stability of the negative electrode material.

claims.claim: DMC has a boiling point of 90.1°C and its decomposition reaction occurs only when the temperature reaches above 200°C during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: DMC is relatively stable with a boiling point of 90.1°C and exists in liquid form at room temperature. The decomposition reaction of DMC occurs only when the temperature reaches above 200°C in the process of thermal runaway occurrence.

claims.claim: At charge/discharge temperatures above 50°C, the effect of cathode material activity on DMC consumption is greater than the effect of increased internal gas production, resulting in lower DMC content in thermal runaway gas compared to 40°C.
claims.quote: the content of DMC in the thermal runaway gas of the battery at 50°C as well as 60°C CTE is lower than that at 40°C, indicating that the effect of cathode material activity on DMC is higher than that of internal gas production when the temperature is above 50°C.

claims.claim: For NCM cells, the onset temperature of thermal runaway (T_onset) monotonically increases from 353 K to 417 K as SOC decreases from 120% to 30%.
claims.quote: T_onset monotonically increases from 353 K to 417 K with SOC decreasing from 120% to 30%. This is reasonable as T_onset is controlled by the SEI decomposition from the anode. A decreased SOC results in an increase in cell thermal stability, and thus increases T_onset.

claims.claim: The gas generation amount at the separator collapse temperature (n_sc) increases slightly from 0.042 mol to 0.052 mol as SOC decreases from 120% to 30% for NCM cells, primarily due to the prolonged duration of Stage II.
claims.quote: the gas generation amount at T_sc, n_sc, experiences a slight increase from 0.042 mol to 0.052 mol with SOC decreasing from 120% to 30%. This difference is caused by the extended duration of Stage II with reduced SOC.

claims.claim: For NCM cells with SOCs of 120%, 100%, and 70%, a dramatic amount of gas is released within 5 seconds during Stage III with a comparable maximum gas generation rate of approximately 10 mol/min.
claims.quote: for 120%, 100%, and 70% SOC cells, a dramatic amount of gas is released within 5 s with a comparable (dn/dt)_max of ~10 mol/min.

claims.claim: For NCA cells, CO rather than CO2 is the main component of the generated gas during thermal runaway, indicating that the oxidizability of NCA cells is lower than NCM and LFP cells.
claims.quote: for NCA cells, CO rather than CO2 is the main component of the generated gas, indicating that the oxidizability of the NCA cells is lower than the NCM and LFP cells.

claims.claim: A slight addition of 0.5% C2H6 to the NCM 100% SOC generated gas significantly suppresses explosivity, making it comparable to the SOC 120% mixture, and increasing C2H6 addition to 4.0% causes the Z-shaped explosion limit to transition to a monotonic trend.
claims.quote: As for C2H6 (Fig. 11 d), its addition leads to a most significant change in the first and second explosion pressure limits, indicating its strong influence on the explosivity of the mixture. With a slight addition of 0.5%, the explosivity of the mixture is significantly suppressed, which is comparable with that of the SOC 120% mixture. Further increasing the additional proportion of C2H6 to 4.0%, the Z-shaped explosion limit transits to a monotonic trend, being more explosive with increasing temperature and pressure.

claims.claim: The Z-shaped explosion limits of the generated gas shift from high-temperature regions to low-temperature regions as the cathode material varies from NCM to LFP and NCA, indicating that NCA cell generated gas is most explosive, followed by LFP and then NCM.
claims.quote: the Z-shaped explosion limits shift significantly from the high-temperature region to the low-temperature region as the cathode material varies from NCM to LFP and NCA. This indicates that the generated gas of NCA cells is the most explosive, followed by LFP cells and NCM cells.

claims.claim: Non-monotonic responses of explosion limit curves with respect to cell SOC are observed for NCM cells, contradicting the common belief that higher SOC always produces more explosive gas; for NCM cells, the explosion temperature moves to higher regions as SOC increases from 100% to 120%.
claims.quote: for NCM cells, the explosion temperature moves to the high-temperature region as the SOC increases from 100% to 120%. Similarly, for LFP cells, the first explosion limit shifts upwards as the SOC increases from 50% to 100%. These are inconsistent with the assumption that mixtures containing higher proportions of highly reactive components, such as H2, have higher explosivity.

claims.claim: The gas generation rate during Stage II (prior to violent thermal runaway) is generally higher for NCM cells with lower SOCs, because T_onset increases with decreased SOC, and higher temperature increases the reactivity of side reactions.
claims.quote: dn/dt is generally higher for cells with lower SOCs during Stage II. This is reasonable as T_onset increases with decreased SOC (see Fig. 3). An increased temperature could increase the reactivity of the side reactions and as such enhance dn/dt.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, LFP cells generate a lower volume of gas than LCO, NMC, and LMO chemistries: 0.4 L/Ah to 1.4 L/Ah versus 1.28 L/Ah to 21 L/Ah respectively.
claims.quote: At 100% SOC LFP cells are shown to generate a lower volume of gas than other chemistries (LCO, NMC, LMO), 0.4 L/Ah to 1.4 L/Ah versus 1.28 L/Ah to 21 L/Ah respectively

claims.claim: NMC LIBs produce more off-gas than other chemistries at 100% SOC, with median specific volumes of LCO 394 L/kWh, LFP 126 L/kWh, NCA 169 L/kWh, and NMC 519 L/kWh.
claims.quote: NMC LIBs produce more off-gas than other chemistries (LCO 394 L/kWh, LFP 126 L/kWh, NCA 169 L/kWh, NMC 519 L/kWh)

claims.claim: Prismatic cells tend to generate larger specific off-gas volumes than other cell forms, with median values of 598 L/kWh (air) and 573 L/kWh (inert), compared to cylindrical cells at 156 L/kWh (air) and 157 L/kWh (inert).
claims.quote: more gas is typically produced by prismatic cells (where median values are 598 L/kWh - air, 573 L/kWh - inert) and pouch cells (502 L/kWh - air, 408 L/kWh - inert) than cylindrical cells (156 L/kWh - air, 157 L/kWh - inert)

claims.claim: LFP batteries release over ten times more HF than NMC batteries normalised against battery capacity: 50 g/kWh to 100 g/kWh for LFP versus less than 10 g/kWh for NMC.
claims.quote: the LFP chemistry typically releases over ten times more HF than the NMC chemistry normalised against battery capacity (50 g/kWh to 100 g/kWh for LFP vs <10 g/kWh for NMC)

claims.claim: LFP cells show a slight downward trend in HF emissions as SOC increases, from approximately 100 g/kWh at 0% SOC to 50 g/kWh at 100% SOC, attributed to higher thermal runaway temperatures reached at higher SOC.
claims.quote: LFP cells show a slight downward trend in emissions as SOC increases (100 g/kWh at 0% SOC to 50 g/kWh at 100% SOC), due to higher TR temperatures reached

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, NMC batteries emit approximately 10 times more CO per unit capacity than LFP batteries: 172 g/kWh for NMC versus 19 g/kWh for LFP.
claims.quote: at 100% SOC batteries with an NMC chemistry emit 10 times more CO specific to battery capacity than the LFP chemistry (172 g/kWh for NMC vs 19 g/kWh for LFP)

claims.claim: NMC batteries show a tendency to release more CO with increased SOC, ranging from 10 g/kWh at 25% SOC to 172 g/kWh at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: NMC batteries show a tendency to release more CO with increased SOC (10 g/kWh to 172 g/kWh for 25% to 100% SOC)

claims.claim: LFP batteries are significantly more toxic than NMC batteries in absolute terms when considering both HF and CO, especially at 0% SOC, with contaminated volumes of 355 m3 (CO) and 695 m3 (HF) for LFP versus 115 m3 (CO) and 51 m3 (HF) for NMC at 100% SOC from a 0.01 kWh battery.
claims.quote: From Table 3 it is shown that LFP batteries are significantly more toxic than NMC batteries when considering both major toxic components, especially at 0% SOC

claims.claim: For NMC batteries, the contaminated volume more than doubles from 0% to 100% SOC due to the large increase in CO emissions, while for LFP batteries it halves over the same SOC range.
claims.quote: Toxicity varies with SOC, for NMC batteries the contaminated volume doubles from 0% to 100% SOC while for LFP in halves

claims.claim: The calculated lower flammability limit (LFL) of LFP off-gas (6.2%) is lower than that of NMC off-gas (7.9% to 9.2%), attributed to LFP having a lower fraction of CO and CO2 while having higher H2 fractions.
claims.quote: the LFP chemistry has a lower LFL (6.2%) compared to NMC (7.9% to 9.2%). This is attributed to LFP having a lower fraction of CO (which has a large LFL) and CO2 (which dilutes the mixture), whilst having higher H2 fractions, compared to NMC

claims.claim: Given the median LFL and gas volumes in an inert atmosphere, LFP batteries breach their LFL in a volume 18% smaller than NMC cells.
claims.quote: Given the median LFL (Fig. 14) and gas volumes produced (Fig. 6) in an inert atmosphere then LFP batteries would breach their LFL in a volume 18% smaller than NMC cells

claims.claim: The greatest increase in gas emissions with SOC is typically seen over the range of 50% to 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the greatest increase in emissions is typically seen over the range of 50% to 100% SOC

claims.claim: For NMC cells (18650, 3.5 Ah), the lower explosion limit (LEL) increases up to a maximum of 10.15% at 50% SOC and then reduces at higher SOC, while the upper explosion limit (UEL) continually increases with SOC.
claims.quote: for NMC cells (18650, 3.5 Ah) the lower explosion limit (LEL) increases up to a maximum of 10.15% at a SOC of 50% and then reduces at higher SOC, while the upper explosion limit (UEL) continually increases

claims.claim: A SOC of 30% is recommended for the storage and transport of LIBs because it corresponds to the narrowest flammability range.
claims.quote: the narrowest flammability range is at a SOC of 30%, hence this SOC is recommended for the storage and transport of LIBs

claims.claim: Overcharging of LFP cells produces off-gas with a composition that is 60% electrolyte when no fire or burning occurs.
claims.quote: overcharging of LFP cells shows that the off-gas can have a composition that is 60% electrolyte (where no fire or burning is noted in this test)

claims.claim: EVs generate double to an order of magnitude greater HF than ICE vehicles: 1.5 kg versus 0.7 kg (Lecocq/Truchot) or 0.7 kg versus 0.01 kg (Willstrand).
claims.quote: EVs generate double the amount of HF than ICE vehicles (1.5 kg versus 0.7 kg), while Willstrand et al. [4] show that EVs generate an order of magnitude greater HF than ICE vehicles (0.7 kg versus 0.01 kg)

claims.claim: A single 18650 cell can lead to a contaminated volume of 100 m3 to 400 m3 based on emitted solvents, CO, HF and other toxic components.
claims.quote: Diaz et al. [50] have shown that a single 18650 cell can lead to a contaminated volume of 100 m3 to 400 m3 based on the off-gas considering emitted solvents, CO, HF and other toxic components

claims.claim: 20 mL of 1 M LiPF6 electrolyte can release enough HF in a 62 m2 room to cause serious permanent health effects.
claims.quote: 20 mL of 1 M LiPF6 electrolyte can release enough HF in a 62 m2 room to cause serious permanent health effects

claims.claim: Solid-state cells show a 10% reduction in total gas production and 30% lower proportions of THC and H2 compared to typical Li-ion cells, while the proportions of CO2 and CO are larger (by 20% for CO).
claims.quote: comparing solid-state cells versus ''typical'' Li-ion cells shows a reduction in gas production (by 10%) and a lower proportion of THC and H2 (by 30% each), while the proportions of CO2 and CO are larger (by 20% for CO) for the solid-state cells

claims.claim: Heat release rate (HRR) does not scale linearly with battery capacity because not all cells burn at once, and caution should be taken when scaling other battery hazards linearly with capacity.
claims.quote: it is shown heat release rate (HRR) does not scale linearly with capacity, because not all cells burn at once. Hence, one should take caution scaling other hazards relating to batteries linearly with capacity

claims.claim: The production of gas specific to capacity is 25% greater for a group of 10 cells compared to one large cell of similar total capacity at 100% SOC, but the difference is negligible at 75% and 50% SOC.
claims.quote: The production of gas (specific to capacity) is greater for a group of 10 cells with a similar capacity to one large cell, the gas production is 25% more at 100% SOC. However, at 75% and 50% SOC there is a negligible difference between the group of cells and a single large cell.

claims.claim: For LFP cells, no fire or thermal runaway is observed at 0% SOC, but at 50% SOC and above, sustained jet fires are present.
claims.quote: for large LFP cells no fire or TR is seen at 0% SOC, but at 50% and above sustained jet fires are present

claims.claim: Overcharge generates more gas than over-temperature or nail penetration abuse by more than 50%.
claims.quote: Overcharge is shown to generate more gas (by more than 50%) than over-temperature or nail penetration

claims.claim: Aged cells show less gas generation because more electrolyte is consumed in cells with less remaining capacity.
claims.quote: Aged cells, with less remaining capacity, show less gas generation as more electrolyte is consumed in cells with less remaining capacity

claims.claim: At initial venting during thermal runaway, CO2 dominates the off-gas composition, followed by hydrocarbons and a minimal amount of H2; during deflagration there are significant amounts of CO and H2.
claims.quote: At initial venting, CO2 dominates, followed by hydrocarbons and a minimal amount of H2 [88,89]... During TR there is a marginal increase in H2, while during the deflagration there are significant amounts of CO and H2

claims.claim: For NMC off-gas at 100% SOC in air, the average major components are CO2 (36%), CO (25%), H2 (20%), and THC (12%), while for LFP the major component is H2 (36%), then CO2 (25%), CO (12%), and THC (11%).
claims.quote: For LFP at 100% in air (see Fig. 13), on average, the major component of the off-gas is H2 (36%), then CO2 (25%), CO (12%) and THC (11%). For NMC in similar conditions CO2 (36%) is the major component, then CO (25%), H2 (20%) and THC (12%).

claims.claim: For large NMC cells (51 cells, 20 Ah to 80 Ah), for vent gas volumes greater than 50 L where combustion was limited, 7 components make up over 99% of the off-gas: CO2 (36.6%), CO (28.3%), H2 (22.3%), C2H4 (5.6%), CH4 (5.2%), C2H6 (1.0%), and C3H6 (0.5%).
claims.quote: Koch et al. found that (for vent gas volumes greater than 50 l where combustion was limited) 7 components make up over 99% of the off-gas volume. These components (and their average volume proportions) are CO2 (36.6%), CO (28.3%), H2 (22.3%), C2H4 (5.6%), CH4 (5.2%), C2H6 (1.0%) and C3H6 (0.5%).

claims.claim: The lower TR temperature of LFP cells makes it more likely that vent gas is emitted without combustion, resulting in a greater likelihood of vapour cloud emission and accumulation and an increased risk of vapour cloud explosions compared to NMC.
claims.quote: due to the lower TR temperature of LFP cells it is more likely that the vent gas is emitted without combustion. This results in a greater likelihood of vapour cloud emission and accumulation, leading to an increased risk of vapour cloud explosions for LFP batteries compared to NMC

claims.claim: The common electrolyte solvents DMC, EMC, and DEC have low boiling points of 90°C to 129°C and high relative evaporation rates (between 1 and 3), while PC and EC have higher boiling points of 242°C to 249°C and lower relative evaporation rates (less than 0.005).
claims.quote: The solvents DMC, EMC and DEC (common in electrolytes) are highly volatile with low boiling points of 90◦C to 129◦C and high relative evaporation rates (between 1 and 3). PC and EC can be considered less volatile with higher boiling points of 242◦C to 249◦C and lower relative evaporation rates (<0.005).

claims.claim: Increased gas generation at higher SOC is attributed to higher electrode potentials and more reactive cell materials.
claims.quote: Increased gas generation at higher SOC is attributed to higher electrode potentials and more reactive cell materials

claims.claim: The total quantity of vented gas from a 4-cell CTP lithium-ion battery module (153 Ah per cell, NCM523 positive electrode) during thermal runaway in a closed nitrogen space was 31.01 mol, equivalent to approximately 1023.143 L at 101.325 kPa and 402.3 K.
claims.quote: It can be calculated that the total quantity of the gases is 31.01 mol, which is about 1023.143 L (101.325 kPa, 402.3 K), and the vented gas per Ah of this type of battery can be calculated as 1.672 L.

claims.claim: The main components of vented gas from the CTP battery module during thermal runaway are H2 (32.85%), CO2 (28.00%), CO (25.25%), C2H4 (8.32%), and CH4 (3.00%).
claims.quote: The main components of vented gas are H2, CO2, CO, C2H4, and CH4. Among these, the H2 content is 32.85 %, the CO2 content is 28.00 %, the CO content is 25.25 %, the C2H4 and the CH4 content are 8.32 % and 3.00 % respectively.

claims.claim: The calculated explosion limit of the vented gas mixture from the CTP battery module under standard conditions is 6.27%–54.14%, which is a wider range and has a lower explosive limit than methane alone.
claims.quote: The calculated explosion limit of vented gases under standard conditions is 6.27 %–54.14 %, which is compared with the explosion limit of common flammable gases, as shown in Fig. 8. As can be seen from the figure, the explosion limit range of vented gases of the battery is larger than that of methane, and the lower explosion limit is lower.

claims.claim: In a closed nitrogen environment, the TRP temperature of the battery module was significantly higher than in an open space system, due to the smaller heat dissipation coefficient of the closed nitrogen space.
claims.quote: The TRP temperature of the closed nitrogen space system was significantly higher than that of the open space system, which was mainly caused by the small heat dissipation coefficient of the closed nitrogen space.

claims.claim: The mass loss of the battery module in a closed nitrogen environment (24.1%–34.5%) was much smaller than in an open space air system (38.2%–43.1%).
claims.quote: the mass loss of the single unit in the battery module is 24.1 %–34.5 %, which is far less than that in the open space air system... the mass loss of the battery module in the open environment is 38.2 %–43.1 %.

claims.claim: The time of pressure abrupt change in the closed space was slightly earlier than the TR trigger time, indicating that monitoring closed-space pressure can provide more accurate early warning of TR onset in the module.
claims.quote: The times of pressure abrupt change are 815.2 s, 914.9 s, 1045.2 s and 1167.0 s, respectively. Triggering times of TR are 815.7 s, 915.6 s, 1046.5 s and 1167.8 s, which is slightly later than the time of pressure sudden change. Therefore, the analysis of the pressure change in the enclosed space can more accurately warn the time of triggering TR in the module.

claims.claim: The maximum vented gas molar rate during TRP of the 4-cell CTP module occurred during the eruption of the 2nd battery, reaching 7.188 mol/s.
claims.quote: the maximum vented gas rate appears during the eruption of 2# battery (7.188 mol/s).

claims.claim: The TRP time between successive cells in the closed nitrogen environment (99.9 s, 130.9 s, 121.3 s) was consistent with TRP times in an open-air environment (129 s, 120 s, 112 s), indicating that the closed nitrogen environment has little influence on TRP time.
claims.quote: It can be seen that the nitrogen environment system in a confined space has little influence on the TRP time.
